preamble: The shift from the voiceless postalveolar fricative [ʃ] (sh) to the palatal approximant [j] is a process of gliding or lenition. This weakening occurs when the turbulent airflow of the fricative is smoothed into a sonorous glide, typically passing through an intermediate palatal fricative [ç] stage.

examples.note: In some Gulf or Levantine dialects, sibilant fricatives can weaken toward palatal glides in specific phonetic environments.
In some varieties, the coda sibilant [ʃ] can reduce to a light palatal glide before certain consonants.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Holes, C. (2004). Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties.
